text:
During the fall of 2010, concurrent measurements of particle hygroscopic growth at 90% relative humidity, cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition were conducted in the Thüringer Wald mountain range of central Germany. Throughout the sampling period, the median hygroscopicity parameters ($\kappa$) derived from hygroscopicity measurements were 0.14, 0.14, 0.17, 0.21, 0.24, and 0.28 for particles with diameters of 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, and 250 nm, respectively. Closure between HTDMA-measured ($\kappa_{HTDMA}$) and chemical composition-derived ($\kappa_{chem}$) hygroscopicity was investigated using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) mixing rule. We found that using size-averaged chemical composition led to a substantial overprediction of $\kappa$ values (by 30% to 40% for 100 and 150 nm particles); however, incorporating size-resolved composition significantly improved the closure. Discrepancies between predicted and measured growth were partly attributed to the evaporation of NH$_4$NO$_3$ within the HTDMA system. Additionally, the hygroscopicity of the organic fraction ($\kappa_{org}$) showed a positive correlation with the O:C ratio ($\kappa_{org}$ = 0.19 × (O:C) − 0.03), providing a useful framework for defining organic hygroscopicity in closure studies.

While $\kappa$ values derived from CCN measurements were approximately 30% higher than those determined from hygroscopic growth (considering the hydrophilic mode), this difference likely reflects the influence of surface tension, solution non-ideality, gas-particle partitioning of semivolatile species, and the effects of partially soluble or undissolved matter. Consequently, extrapolating particle properties from sub-saturated HTDMA data to the point of activation requires caution. Finally, a closure study between CCN-measured ($\kappa_{CCN}$) and chemically derived ($\kappa_{CCN, chem}$) values demonstrated that $\kappa_{CCN}$ can be accurately predicted using size-resolved chemical information and the ZSR mixing rule.
